coredata.dc:title: Novel visible-light-driven direct Z-scheme Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterojunctions for enhanced photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Ke, Xiaochun
Zhang, Jinfeng
Dai, Kai
Lv, Jiali
Liang, Changhao
coredata.dc:description: Construction of high-stability heterojunctions for two-dimensional composites with enhanced photocatalytic performance is regarded as a promising approach to treat wastewater. Herein, the novel direct Z-scheme Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterostructured photocatalyst was rationally fabricated via a simple two-step solution method, demonstrating importantly enhanced activity of methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. Particularly, the degradation rate constant (k app) of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterojunction is as high as 0.0715 min−1, which is 1.55 and 35.76 times higher than that of pristine Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8, respectively. Additionally, 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite has excellent stability and its degradation efficiency can still maintain about 90% after four cycles. Based on the reasonable characterizations and analyses for the heterostructure, the enhanced photocatalytic property and excellent stability can be ascribe to the formation of a novel direct Z-scheme heterojunction between Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4, which presents high-efficient charge carriers separation, improved redox capacity and excellent visible light absorption. This work offers a new strategy to design the direct Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst in the degradation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of high-stability heterojunctions for two-dimensional composites with enhanced photocatalytic performance is regarded as a promising approach to treat wastewater. Herein, the novel direct Z-scheme Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterostructured photocatalyst was rationally fabricated via a simple two-step solution method, demonstrating importantly enhanced activity of methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. Particularly, the degradation rate constant (k app) of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterojunction is as high as 0.0715 min−1, which is 1.55 and 35.76 times higher than that of pristine Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8, respectively. Additionally, 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite has excellent stability and its degradation efficiency can still maintain about 90% after four cycles. Based on the reasonable characterizations and analyses for the heterostructure, the enhanced photocatalytic property and excellent stability can be ascribe to the formation of a novel direct Z-scheme heterojunction between Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4, which presents high-efficient charge carriers separation, improved redox capacity and excellent visible light absorption. This work offers a new strategy to design the direct Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst in the degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, the rapid development of industry and the rising of population have brought serious water pollution problems, especially the existence of heavy metals and organic pollutants in water [1–8]. Hence, seeking for a high-efficiency solution to settle the wastewater is significantly urgent for human health [9–11]. Satisfactorily, semiconductor photocatalysis is considered as one of the non-polluted and efficient technologies to purify wastewater, which can fully utilize the sunlight to remove the organic pollutants [12–19]. Up to now, semiconductor photocatalysts, such as Ag2MoO4 [20,21], Ag3PO4 [22,23], BiOI [24,25], BiOBr [26,27], Bi2MoO6 [28–30], ZnO [31–33], TiO2 [34,35], Cu2O [36,37] and g-C3N4 [38,39], have been broadly applied in degrading various organic pollutants [40–42].

Among those photocatalysts, Ag3PO4 with suitable bandgap (Eg ∼2.3 eV) has aroused widely attention for its advantages including good photodegradation property, low cost, non-toxicity and high redox potential [43]. However, the performance of single Ag3PO4 photocatalyst is greatly restricted by the easy recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, the low visible-light absorption and the weak recycle stability [44–46]. The construction of the heterojunction with other appropriate semiconductor materials has been demonstrated to be the most effective strategy for enhancing the Ag3PO4 photocatalytic activity.

In recent years, the novel Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts have attracted widespread interest of many researchers. Compare with the traditional type-II heterojunction photocatalysts, the Z-scheme heterostructures achieve higher redox capacity and more effective separation of charge carriers. In the Z-scheme heterojunction system, the photo-induced electrons on the conduction band (CB) of photocatalyst A will transfer to valence band (VB) of photocatalyst B. Ultimately, the photoexcited electrons and holes accumulate on the VB of photocatalyst A and photocatalyst B, respectively [47–49]. For instance, Huang and his partners fabricated Ag3PO4/NiFe2O4 composite with outstanding photodegradation activity via a green and simple hydrothermal method [50]. Li and co-workers have reported that the Z-scheme g-C3N4/Ag/Ag3PO4 nanocomposite exhibited the enhanced performance of removing Rhodamine B molecules in wastewater [51]. Wan et al. successfully synthesized a novel Z-scheme Ag3PO4 nanoparticle/MoS2 quantum dot/few-layer MoS2 nanosheet photocatalyst with high activity and photostability[52]. In addition, other Ag3PO4-based composite materials including AgCl/Ag3PO4 [53], TiO2/Ag3PO4 [54], Ag3PO4/BiVO4 [55,56], graphene oxide/Ag3PO4 [57–59], Ag3PO4/WS2 [60] and so on had been largely investigated. In our previous work, our group also reported the novel porous g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 nanocomposite by using a facile and green approach [61]. According to above discussion, the improvement of photocatalytic degradation performance of Ag3PO4-based composites can be attributed to the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction, which can facilitate the separation and transfer of photoinduced charge and increase the absorption range of visible light.

Zinc vanadates, including Zn3V2O8, ZnV2O6, Zn2V2O7 and ZnV2O4, have broad application in the field of battery, film, capacitor and photocatalysis [62–66]. Among them, Zn3V2O8 with suitable band structure and low cost is considered to be an ideal catalyst for photodegradation. However, the wide band gap and fast recombination of holes and electrons resulted in its low degradation efficiency [67]. Consequently, it's useful to combine Zn3V2O8 with other photocatalytic materials to improve its performance. In this paper, a novel direct Z-scheme Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterojunction was constructed by a simple two-step solution method at room temperature. The prepared Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites exhibited outstanding photodegradation activity and photostability over the removal of methylene blue (MB) compared with pure Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4. Based on the detailed experimental characterization and analysis, a possible direct Z-scheme mechanism about the degradation behaviors was proposed.

Ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), Zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), ammonium hydroxide (NH3⋅H2O), nitric acid (HNO3), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4) were bought from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). MB, isopropanol (IPA), L-ascorbic acid (LAA), nafion, ethanol, triethanolamine (TEOA) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Corp. (China). And deionized water (DW 18.25 MΩ) was used for all the experiment.

Zn3V2O8 was synthesized via a simple coprecipitation and calcination way. Specially, 0.234 g NH4VO3 was dissolved in 148 mL DW, then 5 mL Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O solution (0.4 mol/L) was added into NH4VO3 solution drop by drop. After the mixed solution was continuously stirred for 6 h, the precipitate was filtered, washed, and then dried in a freeze dryer for 12 h. The dried sample was ground and put into a tube furnace to cacline at 773 K in air for 300 min with a ramp rate of 5 K/min. Finally, the powder of Zn3V2O8 was successfully obtained.

AgNO3 (0.4504 g) was added into 20 mL DW and stirred for 30 min, then the pH = 10 NH3⋅H2O was added dropwise to AgNO3 solution until the mixed solution turned from turbidity to clarification. Then a certain amount of Zn3V2O8 was added into the mixture and sonicated for 10 min. The suspension was stirred continuously for another 20 min. Subsequently, an appropriate amount of Na2HPO4 solution was added into the suspension and stirred for 1 h. After that, the pH of the aqueous solution was adjusted to 7 with 1.0 M HNO3. Finally, the production was washed with 18.25 MΩ DW and dried at 213 K for 12 h. Besides, the Ag3PO4 was also prepared by the same method without Zn3V2O8.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD Rigaku D/MAX 24000) was applied to investigate the crystal structures of Zn3V2O8, Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites. The morphologies and structures of samples were observed by FESEM (Hitachi S5500) and TEM(JEOL JEM-2010). The XPS (Thermo ESCALAB 250) and FT-IR (Nicolet 6700) spectroscopy were used to record chemical status and surface elemental composition of photocatalysts. Specific surface area values were tested by the multipoint Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET ASAP 2010). The optical performance of as-synthesized samples were studied by the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS PerkinElmer Lambda 950) and photoluminescence (PL FLS920). Electrochemical properties of catalysts were measured by an electronchemical workstation with a common 3 electrodes system (Shanghai Chenhua CHI-660D).

The photocatalytic activity of as-fabricated catalysts was tested under 50 W 410 nm LED light. Specially, 30 mg catalyst was dissolved into the MB solution (30 mL, 50 mg/L), and the suspension was continuously stirred during the entire photocatalytic degradation experiment. The illumination time interval was set as 10 min. After each illumination, the MB solution was centrifuged and tested. The degradation efficiency (η) and the apparent pseudo-first-order kinetics (k app) of photocatalysts can be expressed as the following equations:

Where: C 0 is the original concentration of the MB solution and C is the absorbance of the solution after the LED light irradiation at time t.

The fabrication process of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites was illustrated in Fig. 1 . First of all, Zn3V2O8 was prepared by adding Zn(CH3COO)2⋅2H2O into NH4VO3 solution drop by drop and stirred for 6 h Zn3V2O8 exhibited a nanosheet with large specific surface area. Secondly, the as-synthesized Zn3V2O8 reacted with the mixture solution including AgNO3, Na2HPO4, NH3⋅H2O and HNO3 molecules at room temperature. Therefore, we successfully deposited Ag3PO4 on the surface of Zn3V2O8, demonstrating the formation of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites.

The XRD patterns of as-prepared Zn3V2O8, Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites are shown in Fig. 2 . The characteristic reflections at 20.82°, 29.56°, 33.22°, 36.46°, 47.88°, 52.68°, 54.93°, 57.33°, 61.70° and 71.85° are assigned to the (1 1 0), (2 0 0), (2 1 0), (2 1 1), (3 1 0), (2 2 2), (3 2 0), (3 2 1), (4 0 0) and (4 2 1) facets of orthorhombic Ag3PO4 (JCPDS No. 84–0510). For pristine Zn3V2O8, the obvious XRD diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ = 18.56°, 27.01°, 29.42°, 30.97°, 34.92°, 35.89°, 43.09°, 57.61°, 60.57° and 62.97°, which correspond to the (1 2 0), (2 1 1), (1 3 1), (0 4 0), (1 2 2), (3 2 0), (0 4 2), (1 6 2), (0 0 4) and (4 4 2) planes with orthorhombic Zn3V2O8 phase (JCPDS No. 34–0378), respectively. It can be easily seen that the characteristic peaks of both Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 are observed in the Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites with no other impurity phases. Besides, the diffraction peaks of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites have no significant shift compared with the pure Ag3PO4, indicating that crystal structures of the composites are maintained during the synthesized process.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the morphology of as-prepared Zn3V2O8 sample displays a nanosheet structure, and the size distribution is not uniform. Interestingly, Ag3PO4 structure can be easily observed in Fig. 3b, suggesting irregular spherical nanostructure with the diameter of 200–800 nm. As for the 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite, the Ag3PO4 nanospherical structures are attached to the surface of the Zn3V2O8 nanosheets in Fig. 3c. In addition, all elements of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite can be detected in EDS spectra (Fig. 3d). As for the TEM image of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4, the micro structures of Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4 could be clearly showed in Fig. 3e. Furthermore, in the elemental mapping images of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite (Fig. 3f), the Zn, V, O, Ag and P elements are well dispersed.

The surface chemical states of as-prepared photocatalysts and the interior interaction between Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 are investigated by XPS. Fig. 4 a showed the full-rage XPS spectrum of Ag3PO4, Zn3V2O8 and 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4. All signals of O, Zn, V, Ag and P are clearly observed in the survey spectra of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4, which is consistent with the EDS result and further verifies the coexistence of Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 in the composite. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum is indicated in Fig. 4b, the O 1s peak of Ag3PO4 can be divided into two obvious bands at about 532.65 and 530.90 eV, which are attributed to two various states of O with hydroxyl oxygen and lattice oxygen, respectively [68]. The peaks centered at 530.98 and 529.88 eV in the spectrum of Zn3V2O8 can be observed [69]. After synthesizing with Zn3V2O8, the peaks of O 1s in 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 are detected between pristine Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8, which is assigned to a combination of them. As shown in Fig. 4c, the measured binding energies at 1044.76 and 1021.61 eV could be ascribe to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 of Zn2+ from Zn3V2O8, which is very similar with that of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4. In addition, the two strong peaks at 524.57 and 516.86 eV in Fig. 4d can be identified from V 2p5/2 and V 2p3/2 of V5+ in pure Zn3V2O8, while the peaks at 523.96 and 516.78 eV belong to V 2p5/2 and V 2p3/2 of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite [70]. XPS spectrum of Ag 3d in Fig. 4e indicates the central peaks at 374.16 and 368.17 eV, which are corresponding to the binding energies of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 for Ag3PO4 [71]. For 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite, these peaks have a positive shift of 0.15 eV towards higher binding energy. In Fig. 4f, the main peak located at 133.24 eV is related to the P 2p in Ag3PO4, whereas the peak of P 2p in the 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 is located at 133.28 eV. In view of the above investigation, it is clear that the down-shifts of Zn 2p and V 2p and the up-shifts of Ag 3d and P 2p in Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 indicate the electron density migration from Ag3PO4 to Zn3V2O8. These results further confirm that the formation of a heterojunction between Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 interfaces, which could greatly improve carrier separation rate.

Fig. 5 a showed UV–Vis DRS of as-synthesized photocatalysts. It is clear that the Zn3V2O8 sample has an absorbance edge of about 400 nm, while the absorption wavelength of Ag3PO4 is shorter than 550 nm. With the increase of Zn3V2O8 content, the absorbance edges of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites exhibit a slightly blue shift compared with pristine Ag3PO4, probably attributing to the direct interfacial contact between Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4. The color transformation of Zn3V2O8, Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4 was inserted in Fig. 5a. The color of Zn3V2O8 is white, and Ag3PO4 is slightly yellow. In addition, the linear transformation absorption curve of Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 were shown in Fig. 5b. The energy bandgap (E g) can be deduced from the equation below [72]:
(3)

)

Where hν, α, E g are the photon energy, absorption coefficient and band gap, respectively.

According to Eq. (3), the E g of Ag3PO4 can be calculated as 2.28 eV, while that of Zn3V2O8 is 3.28 eV. Moreover, the valence band (E VB) and conduction band (E CB) of a semiconductor can be achieved by the following formulas [73]:
(4)
E V B = X −
E

+ 0.5

(5)

V B

Where E e is the energy of free electrons to hydrogen scale, and X is the absolute electronegativity of photocatalyst. For pristine Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4, the X values are 4.75 and 5.96 eV [74,75], respectively. Thus the E CB of Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 were calculated to be 0.32 and −1.34 eV, and the corresponding E VB were 2.60 and 1.84 eV, respectively.

Fig. 6 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and S BET of different photocatalysts. These isotherms of samples have stronger interaction with N2 when they are close to the Y-axis at the lower pressure region. Furthermore, hysteresis loops are presented at higher pressure region for all catalysts. So, these isotherms are classified as type IV with H3 hysteresis loops. And the S BET of Ag3PO4 is only 2.9 m2/g, which is much lower than that of Zn3V2O8 (25.5 m2/g). Apparently, after combining the Ag3PO4 with Zn3V2O8, the S BET of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 system increases.

The FT-IR spectra of Ag3PO4, Zn3V2O8 and Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites are displayed in Fig. 7 . A broad absorption band at about 3500–3000 cm−1 and a weak peak at 1661 cm−1 can be easily observed in the spectra, which are attributed to the vibrations of O–H of water molecules on the surface of samples. It has been reported that adsorbed water molecules can be converted into strongly oxidized hydroxyl groups for degradation of organic pollutants. Besides, the strong peak at around 1074 cm−1 observed, which could be assigned to vibrations of PO4 3−. As for Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites, some obvious vibration bands of Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 are appeared in the spectrum, suggesting the formation of the heterojunction between Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8.

In order to reveal the separation and recombination of photogenerated hole-electron pairs of catalysts, Fig. 8 exhibits the PL spectra of Zn3V2O8, Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites. The strongest emission peak was centered at about 550 nm for the pristine Zn3V2O8, which displayed rapid recombination of electrons and pairs. However, the peak intensity of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites have significantly decreased compared with pure Zn3V2O8, indicating that Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite photocatlysts have more efficient separation of photoinduced charge carriers, which can be attributed to the formation of heterojunction between Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4.

To clarify the photocatalytic mechanism, the photocurrent responses of the prepared photocatlysts under visible light were shown in Fig. 9 . It can be observed that the 20% Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite exhibited more increased photocurrent density than that of the single component samples (Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4) and other proportions of composites, implying that more photogenerated charge carriers are produced and the photo-excited electron-hole pairs separation efficiency is improved for Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 [76,77]. Therefore, it is consistent with the photocatalytic degradation activity of photocatalysts.

The typical MB photodegradation activity experiments of as-prepared catalysts were carried out under 410 nm LED light illumination. From what we can see in Fig. 10 a, the degradation performances of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites have obviously enhanced through comparison of the degradation curves of pristine Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4. Among the obtained Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites with different Zn3V2O8 contents, 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 exhibits the highest photocatalytic property. As indicated in Fig. 10b, the linear relationship of photocatalysts between lnC 0 /C and t confirms that the photocatalytic reaction is indeed pseudo-first-order. According to Eq. (2) and Fig. 10b, the photodegradation rate constant (k app) of various catalysts were shown in Fig. 10c. The highest k app of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composites is 0.0715 min−1, which is 1.55 and 35.76 times as high as that of pure Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8, respectively. Table 1 lists photocatalytic activities of some reported photocatalysts. The photocatalytic stabilities of Zn3V2O8, Ag3PO4 and 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite were studied in Fig. 10d. Clearly, 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 has adequate stability and its degradation efficiency can still maintain about 90% after four cycles. Above all, the enhanced photocatalytic performance and stability of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 can be attributed to the intimate contact on the interface of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite, which can greatly facilitate the migration of charge carriers.

To further explore the dominant reactive specie in photodegradation, trapping experiments of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite were carried out. In this work, IPA (0.25 M, 2 mL), TEOA (0.13 M, 2 mL) and LAA (50 mg) were employed as scavengers for •OH, h+ and •O2 −, respectively [20]. The experimental results were exhibited in Fig. 11 . And it is observed that the addition of LAA and IPA exerted a negative effect on the degradation activity of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite system. On the contrary, the addition of TEOA in the system resulted in a positive contribution in the photocatalytic reaction process. Therefore, it is deduced that h+, •OH and •O2 − are the main active radicals contributing to the elimination of MB solution, whereas h+ serves as a key factor.

Based on all the aforementioned analysis, a reasonable Z-scheme photodegradation mechanism of Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite is proposed in Fig. 12 . For Ag3PO4, the VB position of Ag3PO4 is lower than the standard redox potential of OH−/•OH (2.4 eV vs. NHE), so the photogenerated holes in VB can oxidize the adsorbed H2O molecules to •OH. And the position of CB of Ag3PO4 is also lower than the potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE). As a consequence, the photoinduced electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 cannot reduce O2 to •O2 − radicals [85]. Besides, the photoexcited electrons on the CB for Zn3V2O8 can react with O2 to •O2 − radicals, while the photogenerated holes in the VB cannot produce •OH because of the negative VB position compared with the standard redox potential of OH−/•OH (2.4 eV vs. NHE) [86,87]. Under visible light irradiation, both Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 in the Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite system are excited to produce charge carriers in their VBs and CBs. These pristine components show poor degradation activity due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated holes and electrons. After constructing the direct Z-scheme heterostructure, the photoelectrons on the CB position of Ag3PO4 can transfer to the VB position of Zn3V2O8 via the Z-scheme pathway, causing effective separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs [88,89]. Meanwhile, the photogenerated electrons and holes are gathered on the CB of Zn3V2O8 and the VB of Ag3PO4 through the heterostructure [90,91]. Hence, the Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite exhibits a strong redox capacity compared to single Zn3V2O8 or Ag3PO4. Specially, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of Zn3V2O8 would reduce O2 into •O2 − radicals, and the holes on the VB of Ag3PO4 would react with H2O to produce •OH. The organic pollutants of MB can be easily removed by •O2 − and •OH serving as reactive species in the process of photodegradation reaction. As a result, the photocatalytic property was enhanced owing to the formation of a novel direct Z-scheme heterostructure between Zn3V2O8 and Ag3PO4 [92], which can not only improve the migration of photoexcited charges but possess excellent redox ability for the degradation of MB.

In summary, the novel direct Z-scheme Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterojunction has been successfully synthesized through a simple two-step solution method. It exhibited high visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance for MB degradation. Specially, the rate constant (k app) of 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 composite in degrading MB solution is as high as 0.0715 min−1, which is 1.55 and 35.76 times higher than that of Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8. Moreover, the 20%Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterostructure displayed importantly excellent photostability for MB degradation under visible light, in comparison to pure Ag3PO4 and Zn3V2O8 component. The enhanced photocatalytic property and high stability of the Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 system can be attributed to the construction of direct Z-scheme heterojunction, which can promote charge carriers transfer, enhance redox ability as well as improve visible light harvesting capacity. This work designs a novel direct Z-scheme Zn3V2O8/Ag3PO4 heterojunction to strengthen photocatalytic activity of Zn3V2O8 in remediation of organic pollutants.
